segments.s483-g41: همه گنج is a noun phrase 'all/the whole treasure.' با آنک introduces a relative/causal clause using the archaic relative آنک ('which/that') with the preposition با; کردش is a past-tense verb 'made/did' with the enclitic object pronoun -ش referring back to گنج, and نهان is the predicate adjective 'hidden.' Together کردش نهان = 'had made it hidden' (i.e. 'had hidden it').
segments.s483-g42: ندیدند is a past plural negated verb 'they did not see' used impersonally here; زان پس is a temporal adverbial 'from that / after that' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after'); کس with the negative verb yields 'no one'; اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world.' Word order (verb before subject) and the enclitic negation are typical of classical Persian.
segments.s483-g43: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; گنج is the source 'treasure.' نهان کرده is a resultative participial construction ('hidden' formed with کرده = 'made/done'), so 'the treasure that had been hidden.' بر کوهسار is a locative modifier 'on the mountainside' (بر = 'on'). The whole phrase supplies the origin of the memento.
segments.s483-g44: بیاورد is a third-person narrative/past verb 'he brought.' با خویشتن is the accompaniment phrase 'with himself' (خویشتن = emphatic reflexive 'himself/oneself'), and یادگار is the direct object 'memento/keepsake.' Word order is verb + adverbial + object, with the subject understood from context.
segments.s483-g33: This is a relative clause introduced by که (that). Word order is clause-final verb: من (subject) + یافتم (past 'I found') followed by the source phrase ز + و meaning 'from him' (classical z‑ + enclitic pronoun), then the demonstrative/qualifier چنین and the noun phrase چار چیز 'four things'. In classical Persian the enclitic pronoun is often attached after the preposition (زُو = 'from him').
segments.s483-g34: بروبر فزونی here forms an adverbial phrase modifying the verb نجوییم. نجوییم is negative present/pseudo‑present (ن + جوی + ‎‑یم) meaning 'I do not seek' (classical 1st‑person ending ‑یم). نیز 'also/likewise' adds that the speaker likewise will not search for anything beyond the stated items. (The token بروبر is archaic/poetic and functions adverbially as 'beyond/over' in the sense 'more than'; it is not represented among the provided sense choices.)
segments.s483-g35: Numeral phrase 'دو صد' (two hundred) precedes the noun بارکش (pack‑animal/porter). خواسته here functions as a perfect/past participle or resultative ('having caused/ordered' or 'called for') and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put', concluding the clause (typical verb‑final order). بر نهاد = 'placed on'.
segments.s483-g36: This clause continues with another numeric + noun phrase 'صد افسر' (a hundred afsar). In poetic usage افسر means 'helmet' rather than 'officer'. ز گوهر is a genitive/partitive 'of () jewels', بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'on that/thereon', سر 'head' is the object/place, and نهاد is the past verb 'placed' (verb placed at the end).
segments.s483-g37: به کوه اندر means 'into the mountain' (به ... اندر = into). آگند is an adjectival/predicative form meaning 'filled' or 'replete', so 'به کوه اندر آگند' = 'put/filled into the mountain'. چیزی که بود is a relative clause 'something that was…' with the copula بود (past 'was') ending the clause.
segments.s483-g38: ز دینار وز گوهر is a coordinated genitive list 'of dinars and (of) jewels'; the first ز marks the material/contents ('of dinars'), the second token وز is an archaic/poetic connector combining و+ز here joining گوهر (the provided sense list does not offer an exact match for that connector). نابسود is a predicate/adjective applying to the preceding 'چیزی' — here meaning 'not fruitless / not to no avail' (i.e. valuable or not wasted).
segments.s483-g39: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. در کوه 'in the mountain' is the locative phrase. شد is the past/becoming verb 'became' and follows the locative, and گنجها (treasures) is the subject; ناپدید (vanished) is predicate adjective/result: 'when the treasures became vanished/invisible in the mountain.'
segments.s483-g40: کسی here in negative contexts means 'no one' (classical use). چهره 'face/appearance' is the direct object, آگننده is a participial/agent noun 'the one who kindled/caused/instigated' (an attributive noun modifying whose face it is), and ندید is the past negative 'did not see' — together: 'no one saw the face of the instigator.'
segments.s483-g1: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial phrase meaning 'thereupon/after that' (ازان = 'thereupon'; پس = 'then'). بفرمود is honorific past 'he ordered/said' (بـ- prefix + فرمود). کان here is an archaic/dialectal particle introducing the subordinate clause 'that ...' (used like modern که/آن); it is poetic. جام زرد is noun + adjective 'yellow cup' with the adjective after the noun as usual.
segments.s483-g2: بیارند is a jussive/subjunctive plural verb form 'let them bring' / 'that they bring' (context: indirect command). پر کرده is a participial phrase 'filled (having been made full)' where پر = 'full' and کرده is the perfect participle used adjectivally. از آب سرد literally 'from/of cold water' (here 'filled with cold water').
segments.s483-g3: همی خورد is a habitual/progressive past construction ('would drink' / 'kept drinking') with همی marking continuous or habitual action and خورد the past stem of خوردن. زان جام زر = 'from that (زان) golden cup' (زان = 'from that'; جام = 'cup'; زر used adjectivally 'gold/golden'). هرکس آب 'هرکس آب' places 'هرکس' (each person/whoever) before the object; the phrase overall means 'each person drank water'.
segments.s483-g4: ز شبگیر تا بود هنگام خواب is a temporal span: ز = 'from', شبگیر = 'evening/dusk' (poetic 'night-coming'), تا = 'until', بود = past 'was', هنگام خواب = 'the time of sleep' ('bedtime'). Word order is poetic but means 'from dusk until it was time to sleep.'
segments.s483-g5: بخوردند is the past plural form of خوردن 'they drank/they ate' (here 'they drank'). آب is the object 'water'. از پی خرمی is a purposive/causal prepositional phrase: از پی = 'for the sake of / in pursuit of', خرمی = 'merriment/delight', so 'they drank water in merriment.'
segments.s483-g6: ز خوردن 'from the drinking' (ز = 'from/of', خوردن = the verbal noun 'drinking'). نیامد بدو 'did not come to him' is an idiom meaning 'he did not suffer/experience' (نیامد = 'did not come', بدو = 'to him'). در کمی 'in lack/scarcity' uses در + کمی to mean 'any diminution/shortage'—so the clause says 'no shortage came to him from the drinking' (i.e. it did him no harm).
segments.s483-g7: بدان is the prepositional phrase 'به + آن' = 'to that' (here split into ب + دان in the word list); ب = 'to', دان here functions as part of the demonstrative phrase. فیلسوف آن زمان = 'the philosopher of that time' (فیلسوف = philosopher; آن زمان = 'that time' / 'of that era'). شاه گفت is 'the king said' (شاه = king; گفت = said) — together 'the king said to that philosopher of the time.'
segments.s483-g8: که introduces the reported content ('that'). این دانش 'this knowledge' (این = this; دانش = knowledge). از من 'from me' (prepositional), نباید نهفت is an impersonal/modal construction meaning 'must not be hidden' (نباید = 'should not / must not' and نهفت = 'be hidden/concealed'); together: 'that this knowledge must not be concealed from me.'
segments.s483-g17: This is two coordinated past actions in compact poetic word order. بر کید بودند is a prepositional phrase + past copula meaning 'they were set upon a plot' (بر + کید, بود[ند] = 'were'); کین جام کرد is a second past clause with an implicit subject ('they') where کرد (past of کردن) means 'made/caused' and جام ('cup') is the object modified by کین ('vengeance/hatred').
segments.s483-g18: A temporal/adverbial phrase describing when: به‌روز = 'by/in the day' (of the day), سپید = 'bright/white' modifying روز, و = 'and', شب = 'night', لاژورد = poetic color 'lapis/azure'. The sequence gives a parallel day/night image.
segments.s483-g19: همی is an adverb 'always/continually' modifying the clause; طبع اختر is a genitival phrase 'the disposition/nature of the star(s)'; نگه داشتند is a compound verb 'kept watch / held a gaze' (داشتند in past). Word order is subject-omitted (they) › object/phrase › verb at the end, as typical.
segments.s483-g20: فراوان is an adverb 'in abundance' modifying the verb; درین (در + ین) is the locative/temporal 'in this' (i.e., on this day); روز 'day' names the time; بگذاشتند is past 'they placed/put/left' (ب‑ prefix + گذاشتند). The object of بگذاشتند is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s483-g21: This is an imperative clause addressed to 'تو' (you, emphatic first word). از مغنیاطیس gives the source 'from the magnet'; گیر is an imperative form 'take/seize' (verb placed after the source phrase here); این نشان is the object 'this sign' (object follows the verb for emphasis in poetry).
segments.s483-g22: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause modifying 'this sign'. او را is the object pronoun with the object marker را (referring back to the sign); کسی is the agent 'someone' placed before the verb; کرد is the past 'made/caused'; ز آهن‌کشان is a prepositional/instrumental phrase 'from/of a lodestone (magnet)'. The word order (object before subject) is common in classical Persian.
segments.s483-g23: به طبع این‌چنین is an adverbial phrase 'according to this nature / by such disposition'; هم is 'also/likewise'; شدست is the contracted classical form of شده است ('has become') and is a present perfect-like result ('has become/is made'); آب‌کش is the predicate noun 'water-sieve' (the thing that 'has become').
segments.s483-g24: ز گردون is a source phrase 'from the sky/heavens'; پذیره here functions nominally/adjectivally as 'receptacle/receiving' (i.e., 'that receives'); همی is an emphatic adverb 'indeed/still'; آب خوش = 'pleasant water' (آب modified by خوش). Together, the clause says the sieve receives pleasant water from the heavens.
segments.s483-g9: که at the start introduces a reported clause/indirect question ('that' / 'which'); 'افزایش آب این جام' is a genitive noun phrase 'the increase/rising of the water of this cup' (افزایش + آب + این + جام). The clause ends with the inverted interrogative/copular pair چی + ست (چیست) = 'what is', an archaic/poetic ordering where چی (what) precedes the copula ست (is).
segments.s483-g10: نجومیست‌گر is an agent-noun ('the astronomer/astrologer'); following that, آلت functions as a predicate noun meaning 'instrument, means' and هندویست (here) qualifies it as 'Hindu' — the copula is omitted (typical in Persian), so the whole reads like reported speech: '(the) astrologer (says) the instrument is Hindu.'
segments.s483-g11: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed order meaning 'thus gave (a) reply' — چنین (thus) modifies the verb; داد is the past tense 'gave' and پاسخ is the direct object 'answer'. The sequence که‌ای introduces the reported direct speech and the vocative 'ای شهریار' ('O king') — که here functions as a conjunction introducing the quoted address.
segments.s483-g12: This is an imperative prohibition addressed to the king. تو is the vocative/subject 'you (O king)'; این جام را is the definite object ('this cup' + object marker را). خوارمایه is a predicate/object complement 'despicable/worthless'. مدار is an archaic prohibitive/imperative form meaning 'do not make/consider' (i.e. 'do not treat/regard X as...'); the copula/auxiliary is omitted and the negative-prohibitive sense is supplied by مدار.
segments.s483-g13: که introduces the subordinate clause. این در is a locative noun phrase 'this gate/door' (در here = 'gate/door'), followed by بسی سالیان 'many/for many years' as a temporal modifier. کرده‌اند is the perfect/present-perfect plural ('they have done' / 'they have been (doing)') — here meaning 'they have been occupied/working/active (at this gate) for many years.'
segments.s483-g14: بدین در is a prepositional/locative phrase roughly 'at this gate' (text separates the elements as ب + دین + در but functionally reads as 'بِ + این در' = 'at/to this gate'); بسی رنجها is 'many hardships' (بسی here is an intensifier 'many/very'), and برده‌اند is the perfect plural 'they have borne/experienced' (here 'to bear' hardships). The clause repeats/clarifies that many sufferings have been endured at this place.
segments.s483-g15: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from'; اختر شناسان is a compound noun 'star‑knowers' = 'astrologers' (اختر 'star' + شناسان plural/agent 'knowers/experts'). هر کشوری qualifies them as 'of every country' / 'from every land' (هر + کشوری = each country/realm).
segments.s483-g16: به جایی که is 'to/a place where' (به + جایی + relative که). The relative clause has verbless predication: بد نامور مهتری = 'a bad/ill‑famed chief' (بد + نامور 'ill‑famed/notorious' modifying مهتری 'chief/elder'); Persian often omits the copula, so the sense is 'where an ill‑famed chief is (present/rules)'.
segments.s483-g25: همی before the verb gives a habitual/continuous sense (‘ever/continually’). آب is the sentence subject. یابد is a 3rd-person present ('attains/gets' here), and چو introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'when...'. گیرد is a 3rd-person present verb 'takes/seizes' and کمی is an adverb/quantifier modifying گیرد ('a little'). The clause order is main clause + temporal subordinate: 'Water (continually) attains when it takes a little.'
segments.s483-g26: نبیند is negative present 3rd-person ('does not see'); its subject is understood from the previous clause (آب). به here marks the means/instrument ('by/with'), روشن qualifies دو چشم ('two clear eyes'), and آدمی is a genitival noun 'of man' (i.e. 'man's two bright eyes'). The whole segment means 'it is not seen by the two clear eyes of man.'
segments.s483-g27: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. گفتار دانا is a noun phrase 'the speech/words of the wise'. پسند آمدش is an idiomatic verbless/compound expression where پسند ('approval/pleasing') + آمدش ('came to him') = 'was pleasing to him' or 'pleased him.' Note the verb آمدش agrees with the experiencer (him).
segments.s483-g28: سخنهای او is 'his utterances/words' (سخنهای plural); سودمند آمدش repeats the same '...came to him' construction but now with سودمند ('useful') as the predicate adjective: 'his words proved/useful were to him.' The order is adjectival predicate before آمدش, typical of poetic diction.
segments.s483-g29: چنین 'thus' sets the manner. گفت here functions as the speech-verb ('said') though the provided sense list is nominal; پیران is the subject ('the elders'), and میلاد را uses را to mark the addressee/indirect object in older usage ('to Milad'). Word order (چنین گفت پیران میلاد را) is topical/poetic: 'Thus spoke the elders to Milad.'
segments.s483-g30: که introduces reported speech ('that'). من is the first-person subject. عهد is 'oath/pledge'; کید here is an archaic/poetic word functioning within the phrase as part of 'I made/assumed a pledge' (the lexical senses listed are closer to 'plot/stratagem', so the construction is idiomatic/archaic). از پی is a prepositional phrase 'in pursuit of/after' and داد را = 'justice' with the object marker را. The overall meaning: 'that I pledged (to undertake) to pursue justice.'
segments.s483-g31: همی gives an ongoing/emphatic flavor. نشکنم is negative first-person singular (ن + شکنم) 'I will not break' (present/subjunctive form used for volition). تا introduces a purpose/temporal clause here ('so that' / 'until'), and بماند is a subjunctive 3rd-person 'may remain' or 'remain' depending on sense. به جای is a fixed adverbial phrase 'in its place.' The clause expresses intention: 'I shall not break [it] so that it remain in place.'
segments.s483-g32: همی again gives habitual/emphatic tone. پیش او ('before/in the presence of him') locates the thing relative to 'him.' بود is the past/primary copula but here helps state existence ('was/should be'); باید expresses necessity ('must/should') and governs the following locative phrase به پای ('at the foot' = 'at his feet'). Together: 'It should always be before him, at his feet.'

sentences.s483-s21: No one in the world ever saw again the treasure that he had hidden.
sentences.s483-s22: He brought with him a memento from the treasure hidden on the mountainside.
sentences.s483-s17: That I found from him these four things, and I will not seek anything beyond them either.
sentences.s483-s18: He set two hundred pack‑animals (or porters) in place, and he placed a hundred jeweled helmets upon that head.
sentences.s483-s19: They carried it into the mountain — something that was of dinars and of gems and not to no avail.
sentences.s483-s20: When the treasures became hidden in the mountain, no one saw the face of the one who caused it.
sentences.s483-s1: Thereupon he commanded that a yellow cup be brought, filled with cold water.
sentences.s483-s2: Everyone drank water from that golden cup from dusk until it was time to sleep.
sentences.s483-s3: They drank water for merriment; from the drinking he suffered no lack.
sentences.s483-s4: The king said to that philosopher of the time that this knowledge must not be concealed from me.
sentences.s483-s9: They set themselves to a plot and made a cup of hatred — by the bright day and the lapis-blue night.
sentences.s483-s10: They continually kept watch on the stars' disposition; on that day they placed many things.
sentences.s483-s11: You, take this sign from the magnet; for someone made it from a lodestone.
sentences.s483-s12: By this very nature the sieve (water‑sieve) has likewise come to be; it receives pleasant water from the heavens.
sentences.s483-s5: That the rise (increase) of the water in this cup is — the astrologer says the instrument (cause) is Hindu.
sentences.s483-s6: Thus he answered: 'O king, do not regard this cup as worthless.'
sentences.s483-s7: That they have, for very many years, worked at this gate; at this gate they have borne many hardships.
sentences.s483-s8: From the astrologers of every country to places where a notoriously bad/ill‑famed chief is (found).
sentences.s483-s13: Even when the water gains a little, a man's two clear eyes do not see it.
sentences.s483-s14: When the speech of the wise pleased him, his words proved useful to him.
sentences.s483-s15: Thus the elders said to Milad: 'I have pledged myself to pursue justice.'
sentences.s483-s16: I will not break it so that it may remain in place; it should always stand before him at his feet.
